critic_raw:
The primary evaluation metric is described as 'the percentage increase in renewal prediction accuracy when using the RNN over logistic regression.' Accuracy as a metric for subscription renewal prediction on an imbalanced dataset (churned users are a minority class) is misleading: a model predicting 'always renew' achieves high accuracy. But more critically, expressing the comparison as a percentage increase in accuracy over the baseline conflates the magnitude of the improvement with the absolute level of performance and makes the result sensitive to the baseline's accuracy floor. A 5% accuracy gain means something very different when the baseline is 60% vs. 95% accurate.

The stratified random split on subscription data is unjustified more deeply than the design acknowledges. Subscription renewal is a forward-looking event—the label is defined as 'renewal status after a specific period following the previous subscription's conclusion.' If user behavior sequences in the training set contain observations from after the test-set prediction horizon, the model has access to future behavioral signals during training. The design mentions controlling for 'shifts in marketing strategies' but not for this look-ahead problem.

The RNN receives sequential interaction features while the logistic regression receives 'aggregated non-sequential features.' This is not a comparison of model architectures—it is a comparison of feature representations. The logistic regression cannot win even in principle because it is given structurally less information. A fair comparison would provide the logistic regression with the same aggregated sequential features, or would provide the RNN with the same aggregated features, to isolate the architectural contribution from the information contribution.

User behavior in streaming platforms exhibits strong user-specific baseline effects: some users watch a lot regardless of content quality, and some cancel regardless of satisfaction. The RNN will learn these user-level fixed effects through repeated exposure to user ID sequences. The logistic regression on aggregated features will not. The observed improvement in renewal prediction may entirely reflect user-level memorization by the RNN, not sequence modeling of behavioral change signals.

The two-year dataset spanning a streaming platform with 'shifts in marketing strategies' means the training distribution is non-stationary. The stratified random split pools all time periods, meaning the model sees future marketing environments during training that it would not have seen in a real deployment scenario. The confound control described—'identifying stable incentive conditions'—is an underdefined post-hoc check, not a prospective control.
all_issues_raised: The primary metric is 'percentage increase in accuracy,' which is uninformative on imbalanced data and conflates relative improvement magnitude with absolute performance level in a threshold-dependent way.

The RNN receives sequential features while logistic regression receives aggregated features, making the comparison a test of feature representation rather than model architecture; the RNN cannot lose by construction.

Stratified random split on forward-looking subscription labels may allow future behavioral observations (post-prediction-horizon) to appear in the training set for users whose renewal events are in the test set.

The RNN's advantage may entirely reflect user-level memorization (learning individual users' renewal propensity from repeated user-ID exposure) rather than genuine sequence modeling of behavioral change.

The two-year non-stationary dataset pooled across time periods means the model trains on future marketing conditions, and the 'stable incentive conditions' confound control is an underdefined post-hoc check.
